title: How Many Calories Should I Eat to Lose Weight? (Complete Science-Based Guide + Examples)
introduction:
If you want to lose weight, there is one question that matters more than anything else:

How many calories should I eat?

Most diets fail because people either eat too little, feel miserable, and quit — or eat too much without realizing it.

The truth is simple: fat loss is not about cutting carbs, skipping meals, or following extreme rules. It is about energy balance.

In this complete guide, you will learn exactly how many calories you should eat to lose weight, how to calculate your personal number, real examples for men and women, common mistakes that stop progress, and how to make the process simple and sustainable.

By the end, you will know your calorie target and how to use it effectively for long-term fat loss.

sections.heading: What Is a Calorie and Why It Matters for Weight Loss
sections.content:
A calorie is a unit of energy. Your body uses calories for everything — breathing, walking, thinking, exercising, and even sleeping.

Every day, your body burns a certain number of calories. This is called your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE).

If you eat:
• More calories than you burn → you gain weight
• The same number of calories → you maintain weight
• Fewer calories than you burn → you lose weight

Weight loss happens when you consistently create a calorie deficit.

This is not a diet trend — it is basic biology.

sections.heading: What Is a Calorie Deficit?
sections.content:
A calorie deficit happens when you consume fewer calories than your body needs to maintain its current weight.

For example:
If your body burns 2,200 calories per day and you eat 1,700 calories, you are in a 500-calorie deficit.

Over time, this forces your body to use stored fat for energy.

A moderate calorie deficit is usually:
• 300–500 calories per day for sustainable fat loss
• 500–750 calories per day for faster results

Extreme deficits may lead to:
• Fatigue
• Muscle loss
• Slow metabolism
• Binge eating
• Hormonal imbalance

That is why balance is important.

sections.heading: How to Calculate How Many Calories You Should Eat
sections.content:
Step 1: Estimate Your Maintenance Calories (TDEE)

Your maintenance calories depend on:
• Age
• Gender
• Height
• Weight
• Activity level

Step 2: Subtract a Deficit

Once you know your maintenance calories, subtract 300–500 calories.

Example:

If your maintenance is 2,000 calories:
• For slow fat loss → 1,700–1,800 calories
• For faster fat loss → 1,500–1,600 calories

This creates sustainable weight loss without extreme restriction.

sections.heading: Example Calorie Targets for Women
sections.content:
Here are general examples (individual needs vary):

Sedentary woman:
Maintenance: ~1,800–2,000 calories
Fat loss target: 1,300–1,600 calories

Moderately active woman:
Maintenance: ~2,000–2,200 calories
Fat loss target: 1,600–1,800 calories

Very active woman:
Maintenance: ~2,200–2,400+ calories
Fat loss target: 1,800–2,000 calories

Remember: These are estimates. The best number depends on your body and activity level.

sections.heading: Example Calorie Targets for Men
sections.content:
Sedentary man:
Maintenance: ~2,200–2,400 calories
Fat loss target: 1,700–2,000 calories

Moderately active man:
Maintenance: ~2,400–2,800 calories
Fat loss target: 1,900–2,300 calories

Very active man:
Maintenance: 2,800+ calories
Fat loss target: 2,200–2,500+ calories

Men typically require more calories due to higher muscle mass.

sections.heading: How Fast Should You Lose Weight?
sections.content:
Healthy fat loss rate:

• 0.5–1% of body weight per week

Example:
If you weigh 160 lbs (73 kg):
A realistic goal is 0.8–1.6 lbs per week.

Losing weight too quickly can increase:
• Muscle loss
• Hunger
• Fatigue
• Rebound weight gain

Sustainable progress beats extreme dieting.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes That Stop Weight Loss
sections.content:
1. Underestimating calories
Many people eat more than they think, especially with snacks, sauces, drinks, and restaurant food.

2. Not tracking consistently
Without tracking, it is difficult to know if you are truly in a deficit.

3. Cutting calories too aggressively
This slows metabolism and increases cravings.

4. Ignoring protein intake
Low protein can lead to muscle loss.

5. Expecting daily scale changes
Weight fluctuates due to water, salt, hormones, and digestion.
Focus on weekly trends instead.

sections.heading: Should You Eat Very Low Calories?
sections.content:
Very low-calorie diets can work short term but are difficult to sustain.

Problems with extreme restriction:
• Energy drops
• Hormones may be affected
• Muscle loss risk increases
• Higher chance of binge eating

For most people, a moderate calorie deficit is the best strategy.

sections.heading: The Role of Protein in Weight Loss
sections.content:
Protein is extremely important during fat loss.

Benefits:
• Preserves muscle mass
• Reduces hunger
• Increases fullness
• Helps maintain metabolism

General recommendation:
0.7–1 gram of protein per pound of body weight.

High-protein diets make calorie control easier.

sections.heading: Do You Need to Count Calories Forever?
sections.content:
No.

Counting calories is a tool — not a life sentence.

Many people use calorie tracking to:
• Learn portion sizes
• Understand food energy
• Build awareness

Over time, you may develop intuitive habits.

However, tracking can be extremely helpful during fat loss phases.

sections.heading: Step-by-Step: Calculating Your Daily Calorie Intake for Weight Loss
sections.content:
Here's a practical, repeatable way to set your calorie target without spreadsheets or guesswork.

1. Estimate your Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) using the Mifflin-St Jeor formula:

• Men: (10 × weight in kg) + (6.25 × height in cm) − (5 × age) + 5
• Women: (10 × weight in kg) + (6.25 × height in cm) − (5 × age) − 161

2. Multiply by an activity factor to get your TDEE (maintenance):

• Sedentary (desk job, little exercise): × 1.2
• Lightly active (light exercise 1–3 days/week): × 1.375
• Moderately active (exercise 3–5 days/week): × 1.55
• Very active (hard training 6–7 days/week): × 1.725

3. Subtract 300–500 calories from TDEE to create your weight-loss target.

Example: a 35-year-old woman, 5'6" (168 cm), 165 lb (75 kg), moderately active:

• BMR: (10 × 75) + (6.25 × 168) − (5 × 35) − 161 = 1,464
• TDEE: 1,464 × 1.55 ≈ 2,269
• Weight-loss target: 1,769–1,969 calories per day

If doing the math by hand feels tedious, plug the same numbers into a calorie calculator or TDEE calculator — they use the exact same formulas.

sections.heading: How to Split Your Calories Into Protein, Carbs, and Fat
sections.content:
Hitting your calorie number is the main driver of fat loss, but macro split affects how hungry you feel, how much muscle you keep, and how sustainable the plan is.

A solid starting framework for weight loss:

• Protein: 0.8–1 g per pound of body weight (about 30% of calories for most people)
• Fat: 20–30% of calories (healthy fats from olive oil, nuts, avocado, fatty fish)
• Carbs: the remainder, mostly from whole grains, fruit, and vegetables

For a 1,800-calorie fat-loss plan on a 160 lb person, this might look like 140 g protein, 50–60 g fat, and 180–200 g carbs. High protein is the single biggest variable people underestimate — it preserves muscle mass, reduces hunger, and burns more energy to digest.

Use a macro goal calculator to automate the split, and log meals in Eati to check that your day actually lands within range. If you struggle with constant hunger, see [how to stay in a calorie deficit without feeling hungry](/blog/how-to-stay-in-a-calorie-deficit-without-feeling-hungry) for food choices that stretch fewer calories.

sections.heading: Adjusting Your Calorie Target Over Time
sections.content:
Your calorie needs change as your body changes. Expecting the same number to work for 12 months is one of the main reasons progress stalls.

A good review rhythm:

• Every 2–3 weeks: compare your 7-day average weight to three weeks ago.
• Drop rate of 0.5–1% of body weight per week: keep calories the same.
• No meaningful change for 3–4 weeks (and tracking is honest): subtract 100–150 calories, or add 1,000–2,000 daily steps.
• Dropping faster than 1.5%/week (after week 1): add 100–150 calories — you're underfueling.

When you hit your goal weight, transition gradually to maintenance by adding back 100–150 calories per week over 3–4 weeks. This slow ramp helps prevent the bounce-back that happens when people jump straight from restriction to pre-diet eating. For more context, read [how long does it take to lose weight in a calorie deficit](/blog/how-long-does-it-take-to-lose-weight-in-a-calorie-deficit) and [what is a calorie deficit](/blog/what-is-a-calorie-deficit).

midArticleCta: Want a personalized calorie target without doing the math? Plug your stats into Eati, get a daily number, and start logging meals to see exactly how your current eating compares.
conclusion:
So how many calories should you eat to lose weight?

The answer depends on your body, activity level, and goals — but the principle remains the same:

Eat fewer calories than you burn.

For most people, a moderate deficit of 300–500 calories per day is sustainable and effective.

Avoid extreme restriction.
Focus on consistency.
Track your progress.
Prioritize protein.
And monitor trends instead of daily fluctuations.

Weight loss does not have to be complicated.

Once you understand your calorie target, fat loss becomes predictable and manageable.

Now the next step is simple: calculate your maintenance calories and create your personalized deficit.
metaTitle: How Many Calories Should I Eat to Lose Weight? Calculator, Examples & Macro Split
metaDescription: Find out how many calories you should eat to lose weight with a step-by-step calculation, real examples for men and women, macro split guidance, and how to adjust as you progress.
targetKeyword: how many calories should i eat to lose weight

faqs.question: How many calories should I eat to lose 1 pound a week?
faqs.answer: To lose about 1 pound per week, create a daily deficit of roughly 500 calories below your TDEE (maintenance). For a 180-lb moderately active man with a TDEE of ~2,700, this would mean eating around 2,200 calories per day. Use a TDEE calculator to get your personal maintenance number first.

faqs.question: Is 1,200 calories a day enough to lose weight?
faqs.answer: For many adults, 1,200 calories is too low. It's often below BMR for men and taller or more active women, and can trigger hunger, muscle loss, low energy, and rebound eating. Most women do better at 1,400–1,700 calories for fat loss, and most men at 1,700–2,100. Use your TDEE minus 300–500, not an arbitrary number.

faqs.question: Should I eat more calories on workout days?
faqs.answer: You can, but it's not required. Many people average their deficit across the week instead, eating the same amount every day. If you do adjust, add 150–300 extra calories on heavy training days (mostly from carbs and protein) and eat closer to your base target on rest days. Eati makes it easy to see how weekly averages, not single days, actually drive progress.

faqs.question: Why am I in a calorie deficit but not losing weight?
faqs.answer: The most common reasons are underestimated portions, untracked oils/sauces/drinks, weekend intake erasing weekday deficits, and reduced daily movement (NEAT). Tighten tracking for 10–14 days, weigh a few key portions, and look at the 7-day average. If honest tracking shows a true deficit for 3–4 weeks with no change, trim another 100–150 calories per day or add more daily steps.

faqs.question: How do I calculate calories to lose weight without a calculator?
faqs.answer: A fast rule of thumb: multiply your body weight in pounds by 12–13 for a conservative weight-loss target (for example, 160 lb × 12 = ~1,920 calories). This works for most people with average activity, but it's less accurate than a TDEE formula that accounts for age, height, gender, and activity level.
